behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends entirely on what a real LLM produces. A mock LLM could return a generic or hallucinated response and pass a unit test. Only a real LLM call can verify the model actually reasons about distinctive email content rather than ignoring it.
behaviors.test_description: Feed a real or realistic email thread with specific, distinctive claims (e.g., 'budget approved for Q2 only, not Q3'). Verify the generated draft explicitly references that specific detail, not a generic restatement.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires real LLM to verify context-awareness. In-memory mock would always pass, hiding the failure that the model ignores email content.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: The consistency-checking logic needs to run against real (or at least realistic) KB data and draft text. A mocked consistency checker could always pass, fail, or skip checks, hiding real failures in the checking algorithm.
behaviors.test_description: Insert a KB entry (e.g., 'Q3 budget approval not yet decided'). Generate a draft claiming the opposite. Verify the consistency checker flags the contradiction and returns it in the result.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: KB can be in-memory or test database, but the actual consistency-checking logic must execute. Mocking the checker would hide failures in the comparison algorithm.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: Whether a disclaimer actually appears depends on the prompt instructions and real LLM behavior. A mock LLM might skip the disclaimer, or a unit test could only verify 'if KB is empty, expect disclaimer' without checking the LLM actually produces it.
behaviors.test_description: Query a topic with zero matching KB entries. Verify the generated draft includes a clear disclaimer (e.g., 'Note: this claim is not verified against our internal records').
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires real LLM call to verify the model follows the 'fabrication prevention' instruction. A mock could hide the failure that the LLM ignores the prompt.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: The approval state machine must be wired to the send mechanism. A mocked approval gate could hide the failure that send() never checks approval status, or that the gate is bypassed.
behaviors.test_description: Test two paths: (1) Call send() before user approval → verify it raises NotApprovedError or blocks silently. (2) After user marks draft approved, call send() → verify the email is queued for sending.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires both the approval state and send mechanism to be integrated. Mocking either piece could hide a missing gate.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: Multiple agentic steps in sequence: the LLM generates a draft, and then a (possibly LLM-based or rule-based) fact-checker analyzes that draft. The output of one step feeds the next. Data flow and intermediate state all matter.
behaviors.test_description: Use a complete, realistic email thread (e.g., customer support scenario with multiple messages and context clues). Run the pipeline end-to-end: read thread → generate draft → check facts → present UI for approval. Verify each stage receives the correct input from the previous stage and the final approval prompt matches the checked draft.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires multiple real agentic steps to work in sequence. Mocking any step (e.g., always return a passing fact-check) would hide integration failures or data-flow bugs between stages.
